bulkNotes: onboarding-profile: Region lines 1-629 are the HTML and a large inline CSS

screens-scanner-contribute: Range 640-930 (+ contribute modal closing buttons through ~933) is pure HTML markup for the onboarding/scanner/result/contribute screens; no JS data tables in this region. Excluded per instructions: all data:image base64 blobs (KiP/SaK/Dr RooT avatars, repeated on most screens), inline SVG icons (stats bar bar-chart/clock/play/globe icons on lines ~772-778), CSS inline styles, element id/class identifiers, the #scan-debug monospace debug pane (developer diagnostic, not user copy), and code comments (e.g. the 'Photograph the label action lives BELOW the box' and 'Primary fallback' HTML comments). Note: several strings (#result-name 'Product Name', #loading-text 'Looking up product...', #contribute-modal-title 'Add to the I500', tracker vals) are static defaults in the HTML that get overwritten by JS at runtime — the dynamically-assembled JS sentences (e.g. detail.sugar.high NHS-recommendation strings, toast/error messages, concatenated nutrition verdicts) live in the <script> block starting at line ~951, OUTSIDE this 640-930 range, and should be extracted from a later range. Line numbers for the contribute photo block (~929-936) and the two modal-btn buttons (~931-932) are approximate because the 300-col truncation collapsed some lines; verify against full file if exact line numbers are load-bearing.

state-nav: Lines 943-1100 are mostly non-UI JavaScript: security helpers (escapeHtml), the `state` object, the v4.10 tester-data pipeline (worker URLs, UUID helper, WebAudio beep helpers, device-id/opt-out localStorage helpers, sendDecisionEvent payload builder). These contain no user-visible strings except those extracted above. console.warn messages ('[v4.10] Decision write failed:', '[v4.10] Decision write error:') are excluded per instructions. Lines 1119-1160 begin pure data/threshold constants with NO user-facing text: DAILY_LIMITS (sugar/salt limits), BASE traffic-light thresholds (salt/sugars/fat/saturated_fat/caffeine green/amber cutoffs), OVERRIDES (diabetes/hypertension condition thresholds), and the start of the SUGAR_ALIASES array (50+ hidden-sugar ingredient names like 'sucrose','maltodextrin','fructose', etc.). SUGAR_ALIASES is a data lookup table of raw ingredient/synonym strings used for detection logic, not sentences shown verbatim to users as copy — NOT enumerated here as a bulk data table. Note: within the disclaimer/footer strings the words 'Buy', 'Put back', and 'Just looking' are referenced as decision-button labels but their canonical button definitions live outside this 930-1160 range.

additive-db: Lines 1160-1860 are dominated by the food-additive reference database, NOT UI chrome. Treat the following as data values, NOT translatable UI strings (do not enumerate/translate row-by-row):

1. SUGAR ALIASES array (lines ~1160-1166 of source): a continuation of a list of sugar synonyms ('date syrup','rice syrup','barley malt','muscovado','demerara','jaggery', fruit-juice concentrates, 'maltodextrine', etc.). Raw ingredient-name data.

2. E_NUMBERS object (source lines ~1174-1497, ending with closing '};'): ~330 additive entries keyed by E-number code (E100-E1520). Each entry has four fields: `name` (chemical/common name e.g. 'Tartrazine', 'Monosodium glutamate (MSG)'), `category` (one of a fixed vocabulary: colour, preservative, antioxidant, acid, emulsifier, thickener, stabiliser, sweetener, flavour enhancer, anti-caking, raising agent, glazing agent, other), `risk` (green/amber/red — internal enum, mapped to user labels at line 1653), and `note` (1-sentence plain-English safety summary, e.g. 'Synthetic yellow dye linked to hyperactivity in children...'). The `note` strings ARE human-readable English and a perfectionist i18n pass might localise them, but per the task instruction to summarise rather than enumerate large data tables, they are flagged here as a bulk block (~330 sentences) rather than listed individually. The `category` value strings are also rendered to the user verbatim via the .enumber-cat-badge span (line 1664/507) — i.e. the 13 category words above are user-visible labels and would need a small lookup map. Risk colour-keys (red/amber/green) are NOT shown raw; they map to the labels captured above (Avoid if possible / Worth knowing / No concern) and to the count words red/amber/green.

3. E_NUMBER_NAME_MAP reverse-lookup (source lines ~1498-1573): a JS object mapping lowercase ingredient-name strings ('msg','aspartame','soy lecithin','baking soda', etc.) to E-number codes. Pure matching data, not user-facing.

4. Detection / regex / haptic / SVG-icon helper code (detectENumbers, getThresholds, getTrafficLight, hapticEnabled, the kipSVG/sakSVG/drRootSVG/checkSVG/warningSVG generators, etc.) contains no user-facing English strings — only code identifiers, regex, SVG path/attribute data, and code comments, all excluded per instructions.

The only genuine UI-chrome strings in this range are the 15 captured in `strings`, all inside buildENumberResultWarning() (source lines ~1610-1640) and buildENumberDecoderSection() (source lines ~1643-1697): the 'E Numbers Detected'/'E Numbers Decoded' headings, the three risk labels, the profile-relevance flags, and the concatenated/templated count-summary sentences.

renderers-scanner-js: Range 1860-2300 covered. EXCLUDED as non-user-facing: (1) All scanLog(...) calls and console.log('[KiP Scanner]', ...) — these write to a hidden #scan-debug element and the dev console (debug instrumentation, e.g. 'startScanner() called', 'Calling getUserMedia...', '✓ Permission granted', 'Using native BarcodeDetector', 'Falling back to ZXing...', '✓ Scanner running', error traces). They are developer diagnostics, not translatable UI. (2) Code-comment strings ('Scan next', 'Done', \"Let's go\" appear only inside JS comments describing button behaviour, not as rendered labels — the actual button labels live elsewhere in the HTML, outside this range). (3) SVG glyph 'SALT' (line 1879) is rendered <text> inside the salt-sachet icon graphic — a decorative brand glyph, not prose; left out per SVG-exclusion guidance but noted here. (4) Inline base64 data:image avatar blobs for kipAvatar/sakAvatar/drRootAvatar (lines 1902-1915) ignored per instructions. (5) Hardcoded technical strings not shown to users: ZXing CDN URLs, SRI hashes, BarcodeFormat names, profileLabel map values ('Sugar','Salt','Caffeine','General' at the profileLabel function ~line 2030 — these ARE user-facing label values but the function definition is just a lookup; capture them where the cards/UI consume them). NOTE: The profileLabel() return map {sugar:'Sugar', salt:'Salt', caffeine:'Caffeine', general:'General'} produces user-visible profile labels but is a data-lookup helper; recommend localising at the profile-card definitions rather than here. No large data tables (additive DB) fall in this range.

lookup-manual-alts: Range 2300-2750 (plus a few lines past 2750 to close out renderAlternatives) is mostly camera/decoder control logic, product-lookup/caching plumbing, SQLite/I500 data-shaping, and OFF API fetch code — the bulk of which is non-user-facing (scanLog/console.log/console.warn diagnostics, code comments, fetch URLs, localStorage keys, and raw data-mapping). No additive/E-number data table appears in this range. User-visible strings are concentrated in three areas: (1) scanner status text (lines ~2336, 2360, 2403), (2) the lookup loading/error toasts (2583, 2660), and (3) the KiP Sugar Detective alert (buildSugarDetectiveAlert, ~2704-2706) and SaK alternatives rendering (renderAlternatives, ~2766-2780). showNotFound(barcode) and showError(...)/showLoading(...) are called here but their own UI copy is defined elsewhere in the file (outside this range). The Sugar Detective body sentence ('...Now you ') appears truncated in the source — the visible string ends mid-clause. SUGAR_ALIASES (referenced by detectHiddenSugars) is a data array of sugar synonyms defined outside this range; its members surface to the user via {aliasList} but should be treated as data, not separately translated UI strings.

result-notfound: Range 2750-3180 (result-notfound region). NOTE: the SaK alert closing copy (result.sak.alert.title/body, lines ~3181-3182 of buildSakAlert) and the showNotFound not-found banners/KiP-SaK speech (lines 3398-3431) plus the showContribute/showContributePhoto modal titles (3442-3459) fall just past 3180 but are the explicit focus of this task ('not-found banners & KiP/SaK speech'), so they are included. EXCLUDED from this range: large additive/E-number database tables (handled elsewhere), Nutri-Score grade letters A-E and NOVA numeric badges (raw data/labels), traffic-light class names (red/amber/green as CSS classes), and the Dr RooT detail-screen nutritional notes (buildDrRootNote, renderDetail, buildAllergenWarning) which begin at line ~3187 and continue well past 3180 — those belong to a separate 'detail' region and were not extracted here to avoid double-counting. No raw E-number codes or base64/SVG path data were enumerated. localStorage keys, console.warn text, and code comments excluded per instructions.

detail-health-notes: No large data table appears in this range. Note that detail.row.* labels (Energy, Fat, Salt, etc.) are nutrient labels used both for the rendered table and as the leading token of the Dr RooT note sentences. Raw values interpolated into the js_template strings (salt/sugar/fat figures, mg/µg conversions, barcode, filenames, ingredient text, allergen names) are runtime data, not translatable content. The 'salt = sodium × 2.5' formula and numeric NHS/WHO reference figures are part of the translatable sentence text. Excluded: code comments, console.log/console.warn text ('[I500] Queued contribution...', 'I500 photo save skipped...', 'I500 queue save:'), the CONTRIBUTE_WORKER_URL, localStorage keys, appVersion 'kip-pwa-2026.04', and the kipExportI500 console-usage comment block (developer-only, not user-facing UI).

ocr-readout-vision: Lines 3680-4095 (region ocr-readout-vision) are almost entirely JavaScript logic: the I500 export/console utilities (kipExportI500, kipI500Stats, triggerDownload), DOMContentLoaded init, the on-device Tesseract.js OCR controller (loader, preprocessImage, recognizeLabel), the Claude Vision Worker OCR path (visionToReadout, populateFromVision, runVisionOCR), and wiring. User-visible strings are sparse and concentrated in the OCR readout renderers (renderReadout, renderStatus, renderFailure, populateFromVision) plus showError/showLoading helpers. No additive/E-number data table is present in this range. NOT user-facing and excluded: all console.log strings (e.g. '[I500] Queue is empty.', scanLog messages, console.table keys total_queued/photos_stored), CSV header tokens ('id','timestamp','barcode', etc. — data field names not UI), download filename templates (kip-i500-queue-*.csv/json, *.jpg), Error() exception messages thrown internally and caught before display ('All Tesseract sources failed', 'Image load failed', 'Worker '+status), code identifiers, CSS/style strings, URLs (VISION_WORKER_URL, CDN/SRI src paths), DOM ids/class names, and all code comments. The 'Energy' row may show kcal/kJ values joined by ' / ' (slash separator, not a translatable word). Indented sub-rows ('of which saturates'/'of which sugars') are stored in source with two leading spaces used purely as an indentation flag (.indexOf('  ')===0) and are .trim()'d before rendering. The showError() message text itself ({msg} param) originates from caller call-sites elsewhere in the file, outside this range.

strings.en: The CheckIT Crew — KiP, SaK, and Dr RooT
For people who want to know what's really in their food.
Hi, we are the CheckIT Crew — KiP, SaK, and Dr RooT. We read what's behind the barcode for you.
Food literacy tool. Not medical advice.
Which language works best for you?

strings.note: button label, also acts as English option

strings.en: Meet SaK. Scan and Know. Point your camera at any barcode and SaK will tell you what's really inside.
KiP! KiP! Ready to scan. Point me at a barcode and I'll tell you what's really in there.
And this is Dr RooT. The brains behind the operation. When you want to understand why something matters for your health, RooT has the answers. No jargon. No judgement. Just the truth.
I'll show you what the numbers mean and why they matter. Tap my detail screen any time you want to go deeper.
Let's try it. Grab something from your cupboard and scan it. This is your first step to knowing what's really in your food.

strings.en: Red = high. Amber = medium. Green = low. Based on FSA thresholds{profileSuffix}.
strings.note: profileSuffix = ' adjusted for your {profileLabel} profile' when a profile is set

strings.en: Source: Open Food Facts. Data may vary. Always check the physical label. Not medical advice.

strings.note: <strong>; followed by comma-joined allergen names derived from data

strings.en: Salt is high at {salt}g per 100g. For hypertension, the NHS recommends no more than 6g of salt per day. Reducing salt intake can lower systolic blood pressure by up to 5mmHg.
Salt is high at {salt}g per 100g. The NHS recommends adults eat no more than 6g of salt per day. Too much salt raises blood pressure, which increases risk of stroke and heart disease.
Salt is {salt}g per 100g, which is medium. The NHS recommends adults eat no more than 6g of salt per day. Worth keeping an eye on your total daily intake.
Salt is low at {salt}g per 100g. Good choice for keeping sodium in check.

Sugar is high at {sugar}g per 100g. For people managing Type 2 diabetes, reducing sugar intake helps with blood glucose control. Research shows every 10% increase in ultra-processed food consumption links to a 17% increase in T2D risk.
Sugar is high at {sugar}g per 100g. The NHS recommends adults have no more than 30g of free sugars per day. That is roughly 7.5 teaspoons.
Sugar is {sugar}g per 100g, which is medium. The NHS recommends no more than 30g of free sugars per day (about 7.5 teaspoons). Factor this into your daily total.
Sugar is low at {sugar}g per 100g. This is well within healthy limits.
Total fat is high at {fat}g per 100g. The NHS recommends adults have no more than 70g of fat per day.
Total fat is {fat}g per 100g, which is medium. Keep an eye on your total daily fat intake.
Total fat is low at {fat}g per 100g.
Saturated fat is high at {satFat}g per 100g. The NHS recommends men eat no more than 30g and women no more than 20g of saturated fat per day. Too much saturated fat raises LDL cholesterol.
Saturated fat is {satFat}g per 100g, which is medium. Saturated fat raises LDL cholesterol, so worth watching your daily total.
Saturated fat is low at {satFat}g per 100g. Good for heart health.
For people managing diabetes, tracking carbohydrate intake is important as carbs have the most direct impact on blood glucose levels.
Protein: {protein}g per 100g. The NHS recommends about 50g of protein per day for adults.
Fibre: {fibre}g per 100g. The NHS recommends 30g of fibre per day. Most adults in the UK only get about 18g.
This is a good source of fibre.
Energy: {energy} kcal per 100g. The daily reference intake is around 2,000 kcal for women and 2,500 kcal for men.

Trans fat: {transFat}g per 100g. The WHO recommends limiting trans fat to less than 1% of total energy intake. Trans fats raise LDL ("bad") cholesterol and lower HDL ("good") cholesterol, increasing heart disease risk.
Trans fat: {transFat}g per 100g. Low level, but the WHO recommends eliminating industrially produced trans fats from the food supply entirely.
Trans fat: none detected. Good — trans fats are the most harmful type of dietary fat.

Cholesterol: {cholMg}mg per 100g. This is relatively high. While dietary cholesterol has less impact than previously thought, the NHS still recommends keeping intake moderate, especially if you have heart disease or high cholesterol.
Sodium: {sodiumMg}mg per 100g (salt = sodium × 2.5). The NHS recommends no more than 2,400mg sodium per day.
For hypertension, lower is better — aim for under 1,500mg per day.
Calcium: {caMg}mg per 100g. Adults need 700mg per day for healthy bones and teeth.
This is a good source of calcium.
Iron: {feMg}mg per 100g. Men need 8.7mg per day, women (19-50) need 14.8mg per day.
This is a useful source of iron.
Vitamin D: {vdUg}µg per 100g. The NHS recommends 10µg per day for everyone. Most people in the UK don't get enough, especially in winter.
This contributes meaningfully to your daily intake.
Vitamin C: {vcMg}mg per 100g. Adults need 40mg per day. Vitamin C supports the immune system and helps the body absorb iron.
strings.note: line approx; near vitamin C block

strings.en: This product scores well overall for nutritional quality.
A reasonable choice. Could be eaten regularly as part of a balanced diet.
Moderate nutritional quality. Fine occasionally but check the details.
Poor nutritional quality overall. Worth looking for alternatives.
Very poor nutritional quality. Best kept as an occasional treat, not a regular choice.
Nutri-Score {grade} ({label}). {advice} Nutri-Score is calculated from energy, sugar, saturated fat, sodium, fibre, protein, and fruit/veg content.
Unprocessed or minimally processed food. These are the healthiest choices — whole foods with nothing added.
Processed culinary ingredients (oils, butter, sugar, salt). Used in cooking rather than eaten alone.
Processed food. Made by adding salt, oil, sugar or other substances to Group 1 foods. Examples: tinned vegetables, cheese, bread.

Ultra-processed food. Industrial formulations with additives like emulsifiers, flavourings, and colours. Research links high ultra-processed food intake to obesity, Type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
For people managing diabetes, reducing ultra-processed food intake is particularly important — studies show a 17% increase in T2D risk for every 10% increase in ultra-processed food consumption.
strings.note: appended when nova===4 and profile is diabetes

strings.en: Hidden sodium detected: {aliases}. These additives contribute to your overall sodium intake and may not be fully reflected in the salt figure on the label.
KiP found this product, but key values are missing
The sugar or salt figures aren't recorded, so KiP can't give you a read. Photograph the label and KiP will read it for you.
This product isn't in the Open Food Facts database yet. You can help by adding it.
KiP! KiP! I found it, but I'm missing the numbers that matter. Show me the label.
KiP! KiP! I can't find this one yet. But you can help fill the gap.

You've just helped close a gap that the mainstream apps leave open. Thank you.
